category_title: High-intelligence student with inattentive-type ADHD working below potential
minimal_context: Gail is taking a Science test in her classroom.
dialogue:
TUTOR: Gail, please put your extra credit poster board away and start your Science test.

STUDENT: (Takes an inordinate amount of time to put her poster board along the wall, then sits down and takes a long time choosing a different pen while watching the girl beside her put her hair in a ponytail holder)

TUTOR: Please focus on your paper and keep working.

STUDENT: (Gets up to use the restroom, spends two minutes rearranging the keys on the hooks, and takes a circuitous route back to her desk after signing back in)

TUTOR: There are 15 minutes remaining in the Science period. Please finish your test.

STUDENT: (Starts writing on her hand, marks one answer on her test, then goes across the room to wash the stuff off her hands at the sink. She comes back and begins to draw on her knee, below her skirt)

options.A: Teach foundational awareness through short practices with an initial focus on breath awareness, belly breathing, and awareness of the body during the science period and other classes.
options.B: Write school-hour medication dosage times into the student's journal, set a phone alarm to remind the student it is time for her dosage, and start an hourglass or egg timer right after unstructured time.
options.C: Conduct functional behavior analyses targeting these purported variables and subject the student's off-task behaviors during the science test to data-based hypothesis testing over the course of the term.
options.D: Organize a set of mini-classes to help the student become a 'Test-Taking Superstar,' focusing on bubbling, reading directions, eliminating choices, double-checking, and the do's and don'ts of test-taking.
